Just now

--

When one talks about the implementation of AI technologies in the pharma industry, the discovery of drugs is usually the first thing that comes to mind. It’s true that the development process of the industry has been greatly boosted by means of analysis of biological data. But there is another place for the implementation of innovative technology — pharmaceutical manufacturing.
With the growing complexity of the environment and increased regulatory requirements, the manufacturers require greater transparency of processes. And here the combination of AI and IIoT becomes useful.
From Passive Operations to Intelligent Manufacturing
The traditional approach to manufacturing processes in the pharmaceutical industry consists of using isolated systems, manually collected data, and periodic inspections. Of course, these are the tools which have helped the industry evolve for decades, but they also can create problems in terms of the timely detection of problems and quick adaptation to changing production conditions.
Modern manufacturing processes produce constant streams of data generated by equipment, sensors, production line, and warehouse activities. The use of AI for the analysis of such data helps manufacturers understand better what is going on inside their facilities at the moment.
The Role of AI and IIoT
The IIoT makes connected devices and sensors capable of gathering operational data during the entire manufacturing process. And the role of AI lies in turning gathered data into useful insights which will enable decision-making.
Some examples of use cases for AI and IIoT are the following:

• Monitoring of production equipment on a continuous basis

• Environmental monitoring (temperature, humidity, etc.)

• Tracking of assets based on RFID and BLE technology

• Greater visibility into inventories

• Batch traceability in manufacturing processes

• Visibility into workforce operations

• Timely detection of issues with equipment performance
Each of the above use cases helps achieve a higher level of connectivity and information availability during manufacturing operations.
Compliance Management Benefits Aside from Documenting
Compliance management continues to be an important issue for the pharmaceutical industry. Accurate documentation of production process, product traceability and Good Manufacturing Practices (GMP) are very important things and take a lot of work.
With the help of digital technologies, a lot of such activities can be automated. Though it does not replace quality teams, technology gives those teams access to better data.
And it means that you can spend less time collecting data and more time improving manufacturing operations.
Increased Visibility Leads to Better Decision Making
Another major problem in the pharmaceutical manufacturing industry is that valuable information resides in various disconnected systems. The equipment performance, the environment, the stock, and the production processes may all be operated separately from each other.
Connecting those sources results in a much broader picture of the production process.
Operational analytics and real-time dashboards let production managers monitor the performance of the manufacturing process 24/7, without waiting for end-of-shift reports and updating manually. It helps to respond to unusual situations in time and increases the overall effectiveness of the process.
Digitalization Requires Attention of People
The technology itself cannot ensure successful digital transformation of the enterprise. Skilled operators, engineers, quality management specialists, and production workers still play a central role in pharmaceutical production.
AI should be regarded as an instrument that complements the expertise of the people involved in the process. With automation of the monitoring process and analysis of massive amounts of operational data, professionals can concentrate their efforts on process optimization and decision making.
Companies that invest in technological innovation and training of the staff usually perform better in the long run.
Forward Looking
The pharmaceutical sector is gradually but inexorably evolving into an environment of smart manufacturing operations. With further advancements in AI, IIoT, edge computing, and analytics, it will become even easier to optimize, enhance compliance, and build resilience within the processes.
It is getting increasingly relevant to comprehend how all these technologies integrate for companies trying to craft their digital transformation strategy. For those interested in learning more about AI-driven pharmaceutical manufacturing, Industrial Internet of Things, and operational intelligence, there are many educational resources available from PharmaFlux AI.
Final Thought
Pharmaceutical manufacturing of tomorrow won’t be defined only by automation. It will rather be characterized by intelligent systems connecting data and enabling smarter decision-making at all stages of manufacturing process.
While implementing AI and IIoT, the key will be in addressing the real operational problems and improving the quality of the products. Those who will successfully blend technology with knowledge are likely to be ready for tomorrow’s pharmaceutical manufacturing.
